\tableofcontents
\section{Introduction}

% Introduction to actor models and their communication mechanism.
Actor models provide a framework for concurrent systems. In these
systems, the main entity is the notion of an \emph{actor}. Actors are
independent computational units that encapsulate the execution environment of
their processing logic. Asynchronous communication between actors occurs
solely via messages. A message is defined as any piece of data sent from one
actor to another. The reception of these messages is handled by the recipient
actor's \emph{mailbox}, a dedicated piece of storage that preserves message
order while remaining separate from the actor's core logic.

% Challenges in actor system message flow management
One challenge in actor systems is managing message flow under high volumes.
Although some techniques such as rate limiting and%
\emph{backpressure routing}~\cite{backpressure} help regulate message rates,
these control mechanisms may also fail under heavily loaded systems. Message
delivery is often assumed to be reliable, but practical implementations
must consider the possibility of message loss and delays from processing
bottlenecks or crashing actors.

% Proposes a solution by decoupling mailboxes from processing actors.
To address some of these challenges, in particular the need to handle message
loss and delays in processing messages, we propose decoupling mailboxes
from \emph{processing actors}, which are the actors that carry out the processing
logic. In this design, communication occurs through message passing to
dedicated \emph{mailbox actors}, with processing actors pulling messages only
when ready to handle them. %
This approach is simple and similar in spirit to Kilim's buffered mailboxes
(multi-producer, single consumer queues) ~\cite{kilim-framework}, allowing us to
give an explicit specification of the message-passing mechanism, where the
mailbox actors remain opaque to both senders and receivers to maintain
compatibility with existing actor frameworks such as Agha's Object-oriented
programming Actor Model~\cite{foundation97agha,taxonomy-of-actor-models}.

% Outlines the benefits of the proposed architectural separation.
The architectural separation of mailboxes from processing actors provides
several key benefits. First, it cleanly separates message management from
processing logic, allowing mailbox actors to specialise in queue management
while processing actors focus on their core functionality. Second, it enables
flexible message scheduling—different mailbox implementations can support
priority queues, batch processing, or custom dispatch strategies without affecting
processing actors. Third, mailbox actors become modular components that can be
modified or replaced independently.

% Discusses the trade-offs and potential drawbacks of the design.
This design may come with some trade-offs. The primary drawback we foresee is
increased system complexity due to additional actors and message-passing
overhead.%
% This manifests as higher resource usage from running more actors and
% potential latency increases when mailbox actors run on different nodes than
% their processing actors.
However, with careful design, these costs can be
outweighed by the benefits of a more flexible system.

% How this paper is organized and what is the contribution.
\paragraph{Contributions}
First, we propose an architectural separation between actors and
their mailboxes in \Cref{sec:model}. This separation enables improved
flexibility and scalability for message management. Second, we formulate
operational semantics in \Cref{sec:semantics} for the decoupled architecture
and its associated explicit message-passing mechanism, formally specifying the
system's design of a variant of Erlang~\cite{formal-semantics-erlang}. This
formal specification provides sufficient grounding to implement a system from
scratch or as a library on top of a compatible system/platform such as Erlang,
and to formalize and prove key system properties such as liveness guarantees and
correctness under concurrent message processing.

\section{Notation}\label{sec:notation}

We use an informal type-theoretic notation. For example, a term $x$ of type $A$
is denoted $\typing{x}{A}$. Capital letters or capitalized words are used for types,
and lowercase letters or lowercase words are used for terms. There is a universe
of types $\U$, so if $A$ is a type, then $\typing{A}{\U}$. We also work with inductive
types presented in the following form:
%
\begin{align*}
  \defequal{A}{\cdots \mid \mathsf{C}(A_1, \dots, A_n) \, ,}
\end{align*}
%
where $\mathsf{C}$ is a constructor of the type $A$, and $A_1, \dots, A_n$ are
the types of the constructor arguments.

We use standard type notation where $\N$ denotes the inductive type of natural
numbers, $\EmptyType$ represents the empty type, $\Unit$ denotes the type with
exactly one term denoted with $*$, $\Bool$ denotes the type with two terms,
$\mathsf{tt}$ and $\mathsf{ff}$, and $\List{A}$ represents the type of
parametric lists. For equality relations, $\defequal{x}{y}$ indicates definitional
equality where term $x$ is defined as $y$, while $\equal{a}{b}$ signifies
definitional equality between terms, and $\propequal{a}{b}$ denotes propositional equality.
$A \times B$ denotes the product of $A$ and $B$, and their terms, tuples, are denoted
with $\langle a,b \rangle$, where $a : A$ and $b : B$. %
A \emph{tuple} $\langle a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n \rangle$ is a generalisation of an
ordered pair to an ordered $n$-tuple, where $n \in \N$, of type $A_1 \times
\cdots \times A_n$, and $a_i$ is of type $A_i$ for all $i$ from $1$ to $n$.
Given a type $A$, we can define the optional type $\Option{A}$ as $A \oplus
$. The terms $\none$ and $\some{a}$ are the
corresponding terms for the optional type when $\typing{a}{A}$. 

Some types are sets, and we equip them with some common set-theoretic notations.
The membership relation is denoted with $\in$, so $a \in A$ is a proposition
that $a$ is an element of $A$. Union between sets $A$ and $B$ is denoted with $A
\cup B$. The difference between $A$ and $B$ is denoted with $A \setminus B$.
The disjoint union of $A_1, \dots, A_n$ is denoted with $A_1 \oplus \cdots \oplus A_n$.
%
% and $\mathsf{inj_i}(a_i)$ are the corresponding injections into $A_1 \oplus \cdots
% \oplus A_n$ from $A_i$ for all $i \in \{1, \dots, n\}$. 

Finally, a function $f$ that takes an argument $a$ of type $A$ and returns a
value of type $B$ is denoted $f : A \to B$ and application is denoted with
$f(a)$. We also use the notation $f[a \mapsto c]$ to define a new function that
is similar to $f$ except that the output of $f$ is replaced by $c$ when the
input is $a$, if $a$ is in the domain of $f$. Otherwise, we extend $f$
accordingly. We overload the notation $\mathcal{N}[a \mapsto c]$ to also apply
to sets, so if $\mathcal{N}$ is a set, then $\mathcal{N}[a \mapsto c]$ is the
set resulting from $\mathcal{N}$ by replacing the element $a$ with $c$, if $a$
is a valid element of $\mathcal{N}$. Otherwise, we extend $\mathcal{N}$
accordingly.

\section{Model}\label{sec:model}

This section presents a variation of dynamic effective communicating systems
\cite{art-engines} by introducing terminating behaviour guarantees while
preserving their actor-like semantics. Building on this foundation, we formalise
engines as refined effective communicating Moore machines, that is, stateful
message-processing units structured around four core components:
a \emph{configuration}, an \emph{execution environment}, \emph{message interface},
and a \emph{behaviour}.

An engine's configuration (\Cref{def:engine-configuration}) includes, among
other things, the engine's location within the system and an optional reference
to its parent engine. The engine's execution environment
(\Cref{def:engine-environment}) contains its local state and an \emph{address
book} of other engines to communicate with, including the engine's own address.
The engine's \emph{behaviour} defines how it reacts to messages that conform to
its \emph{message interface} (\Cref{def:message-interface}) ---a sum type of
message types that the engine can process. More precisely, the engine's
behaviour is described through a set of \emph{guarded actions}
(\Cref{def:guarded-action}) consisting of pre-condition/action pairs. When an
engine receives a message, it checks which of these guarded actions have their
pre-conditions satisfied and executes the corresponding actions. 
% the following qualifier may not needed after all.
% An action is considered
% \emph{activated} if it belongs to a guarded action whose guard predicate is
% satisfied. 
As an avid reader might note, different strategies could exist for selecting
which action to execute when multiple guards are satisfied or have competing
priority. While this guard selection problem falls outside our model's scope,
alternative strategies could be defined drawing from approaches used in
\textsc{Prolog} implementations and Dijkstra's guarded command
language~\cite{dijkstra-guarded-commands}. We provide one such strategy (see
\Cref{rule:b-guard-strategy}), though this is not essential to the model
presented here, as readers can define alternative strategies according to their
needs.

\begin{remark}
  The engine behaviour specification described above resembles the correct-by-construction
  approach used in event-driven systems like Event-B~\cite{dupont:tel-04165215},
  where system transitions are governed by preconditions on the current state
  of the machine and corresponding actions that are executed when their
  preconditions are met. The preconditions are often written in
  non-deterministic first-order logic, and the actions are defined using a
  language derived from process calculi, which often includes operations such as
  parallel composition, a null operation, sequencing, nondeterministic choice,
  and recursion.
  %Here, we have a more restricted set of actions, as will be seen in \Cref{def:effects}.
\end{remark}

% We need a nice picture here; this is (not) a definition? 
The model assumes an ambient \emph{system} $\SystemState$ mainly consisting of a
set of \emph{nodes}~$\SetOfNodes$, a set of \emph{engines}~$\SetOfEngines$,
following a specific \emph{message-passing mechanism} that will be described
later, among other components.


Engines are distributed across the nodes of the system, with each engine being
associated with a unique node. Each entity in the system has a unique
identifier, allowing nodes and engines to be denoted with $\node{i}$ and
$\engine{j}$ respectively, where $i, j \in \N$.

Following the message-passing paradigm, the message-passing mechanism enforces
policies for message delivery and flow control. This mechanism operates on a set
of \emph{messages} which we denote with $\SetOfMessages$. Each message contains
information about its sender and receiver.

To reason about the system, we use a state machine that evolves in discrete
steps. So, there is a notion of \say{previous} and \say{next} for each entity
with respect to the system, including the set of messages $\SetOfMessages$. The
steps are denoted using superscripts, so $\SystemState^i$ is the $i$-th step of the
system, and $\SetOfMessages^i$ is the set of messages in the $i$-th step.

% Within the system, each entity has a unique identifier. However, engines can also be
% referenced by name rather than by address. Engine names provide a more legible way
% to refer to engines in the system, making them easier to work with. Since names
% are unique and unambiguous, they can be used interchangeably with addresses when
% referring to engines.


\subsection{Addresses}\label{sec:addresses-and-names}
\begin{definition}[Address]%
\label{def:address}

An \textbf{address} is a pair $\equal{a}{\loc{n}{\iota}}$ of type
$\Address$ where:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $n : \mathsf{NodeID}$ is the identifier of the node where the engine
    resides, and
    \item $\iota : \mathsf{UID}$ is the engine's unique identifier within the node.
\end{itemize}
We define projection functions $\mathsf{node}{a}$ and $\uid{a}$ to
extract the respective components of an address.
\end{definition}

\begin{axiom}\label{ax:address}
Each engine is uniquely identified by an address in the system.
\end{axiom}

\subsection{Message structure}\label{sec:message-structure}

Engine communication occurs through typed messages.
\begin{definition}[Message]\label{def:messages}
A message is a triple $\equal{m}{\msgpacket{s}{t}{v}}$ where:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $s$ is the sender address,
    \item $t$ is the target address, and
    \item $v$ is a payload of type $\msgtype{j}$ which is the message type
    the target engine $\engine{j}$ can process.
\end{itemize}
We define projection functions $\sender{m}$, $\target{m}$, and
$\mathsf{msg}(m)$ to extract the respective components of a message.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[Message Interface]\label{def:message-interface}%
The \textbf{message interface} of an engine $\engine{j}$ is the sum type
$\msgtype{j}$ where the type constructors address the different message types
that the engine can process.
\end{definition}

To refer to an arbitrary message type \emph{within the system}, we use the type
$\Msg$, the sum type of all message interfaces, and therefore the valid
message types, that is, $\defequal{\Msg}{\bigoplus_{j} \msgtype{j}}$
where $j$ ranges over all engines in the system. To simplify the notation, we
assume message type subtyping, i.e., $\msgtype{j} : \Msg$
for all $j \in \SetOfEngines$.

% An alternative approach defines message interfaces through untagged unions, permitting
% overlapping message types across engines. This is consistent with our approach,
% enabling shared message constructors between engines capable of processing them,
% though it diverges from our system's concrete implementation in Juvix.

\subsection{Engine components}\label{sec:engine-components}

An engine is a tuple of components which may contain immutable data such as
their name and parent. Although, other information such as its location, status,
and environment can change during the engine's lifecycle. For example, a system
might relocate an engine to a different location when computational costs in its
current location become prohibitive and more favourable resource availability
exists elsewhere, although for simplicity we assume that engines always remain
on the same node.

\begin{definition}[Engine Lifecycle]\label{def:engine-status}
The \textbf{status} of an engine $\engine{i}$, of type $\EngineStatus$, determines
the engine's operational mode.
\begin{equation}\label{eq:engine-status}
\begin{aligned}
\typedef%
  {\EngineStatus}%
  {    &\quad \ready{\Option{\msgtype{i} \to \Bool}} \\%
  \mid & \quad \busy{\msgtype{i}} \\%
  \mid & \quad \terminated  \, .
  }
\end{aligned}
\end{equation}
% tikz
\begin{figure}%[!hbp]
\centering
\[\begin{tikzcd}
	& {\textcolor{darkgreen}{\ready{f}}} \\
	{\textcolor{darkred}{\terminated}} && {\textcolor{darkorange}{\busy{m}}}
	\arrow[from=1-2, to=2-1]
	\arrow[from=1-2, to=2-3, bend right]
	\arrow[from=2-3, to=1-2, bend right]
	\arrow[from=2-3, to=2-1, bend left]
\end{tikzcd}\]%
\caption{The engine life cycle: every engine starts in \ready{f} state waiting
for a message where $f$ is an optional predicate that communicates which
messages the engine is interested in processing. Once it reacts to a message, it
changes to \busy{m} where $m$ is the message to which the engine reacts to.
Then, depending on its logic, the engine may either return to \ready{f'} for
some optional predicate $f'$ or \terminated{}, meaning the engine will not process
any more messages.}
\label{fig:engine-status}
\end{figure}
\end{definition}

As illustrated in \Cref{fig:engine-status}, an engine is said to be \ready{f} if
it can process a message. The additional argument $f$ is an optional predicate
that shares the preferences of the engine to process a particular message type.
For example, this function $f$ could always return true, meaning the engine is
interested in all messages at the moment. Being \busy{m} means the engine is currently
processing the message $m$. And \terminated means the engine will not process
any more messages. In such a case, the system will remove the engine in the next
\emph{step}, and disregard any messages sent to it.

An engine's operation requires two fundamental components: a
\emph{configuration} and an \emph{environment}. The implicit initial state of an
engine then emerges from these two components. Precisely, the configuration
contains immutable data such as the engine's parent reference, and
operational mode. We first formalise this operational mode concept to establish
precise semantics.

\begin{definition}\label{def:engine-mode}
The \textbf{operational mode} of an engine $\engine{j}$, of type $\EngineMode$,
determines the engine's operational mode, a tag used by the system to
distinguish the purpose of the engine.
\begin{equation}\label{eq:engine-mode}
\typedef{\EngineMode}{\process \mid \mail}.
\end{equation}
\end{definition}

As we will see later, the operational mode of an engine determines how the
system routes messages to appropriate engines. Those configured as mailboxes
receive special treatment within the message-passing mechanism. Since the
operational mode becomes immutable once an engine is created, this critical
attribute goes into the engine's configuration.

\begin{definition}\label{def:engine-configuration}%
The \textbf{configuration} of an engine $\engine{j}$, often denoted by
$\config{j}$, is a tuple $\configval{p}{\mode}$ where %
$p$ is an optional reference (address) of the engine's parent, and %
$\mode$ is the engine's operational mode as in \Cref{def:engine-mode}. %
The inferred type of the configuration is denoted with $\configtype{j}$, indicating
the configuration of engine $\engine{j}$.
\end{definition}

\begin{remark}
Similar to message interfaces, we collect all configurations into a single type,
$\Cfg$, the sum type of all configuration types. Subtyping for
configurations means that the configuration of an engine $j$, $\configtype{j}$,
is also a type of $\Cfg$.
\end{remark}

Recall that engines are isolated from each other and process messages
independently. However, an engine's configuration alone is insufficient for
proper operation since some form of state is required. Therefore, each engine
encapsulates its own state as part of its execution context, which we refer to
as the engine's \emph{environment}.

\begin{definition}\label{def:engine-environment}

Given a type $S$ representing the local state of an engine $\engine{j}$, an
execution \textbf{environment} of $\engine{j}$ is a tuple %
$\equal{\localenv{j}}{\localenvval{s}{l}}$ of type $\Env(S)$, where $s$ is of
type $S$, and $l$ is the engine's address book, a non-empty set containing the
names of other engines, including its own.
\end{definition}

\subsection{Execution directives}\label{sec:execution-directives}

The behaviour of an engine is a description of what it does when it
reacts to a message. This description of computation is via an effect system
consisting of commands to the system to be executed at runtime.

\begin{definition}[Effect]\label{def:effects}%
Given a type $S_j$ representing the local state of an engine~$\engine{j}$, an
\textbf{effect} is of type $\effecttype{j}{S_j}$, which considers all the possible
ways an engine can influence the system by reacting to a message.

\begin{equation}\label{eq:effects}
\begin{aligned} %TODO: review this grammar
\typedef{%
   \effecttype{j}{S_j}
}{%
      &\quad \send{\Address}{\Msg} & &%
        \text{(message dispatch)} \\
\mid & \quad \update{\envtype{j}{S_j}} & &%
        \text{(engine state modification)} \\
\mid & \quad \spawn{\configtype{i}}{\envtype{i}{S_i}} & &%
        \text{(engine creation)} \\
\mid & \quad \mfilter{%
                \Option{\msgtype{j} \to \Bool}
              }& &%
        \text{(new engine mailbox filter)} \\
\mid & \quad \chain{\effecttype{j}{S_j}}{\effecttype{j}{S_j}} & &%
        \text{(action sequencing)} \\
\mid & \quad \terminate & &%
        \text{(engine shutdown)} \\
\mid & \quad \noop & &%
        \text{(null operation)\, .}
}
\end{aligned}
\end{equation}
\end{definition}

% TODO: mention how this induces a monad.
% also mention CCS?
\begin{figure}%[!htbp]
% https://q.uiver.app/#
\[\begin{tikzcd}
	{} & {(m, \config{j},\localenv{j})} & {} \\
	\\
	{r_1} & \cdots & {r_k} \\
	{e_1:\effecttype{j}{S_j}} & \cdots & {e_k:\effecttype{j}{S_j}}
	\arrow[curve={height=24pt}, shorten <=24pt, shorten >=24pt, dashed, no head, from=1-1, to=1-3]
	\arrow["{g_1}"', maps to, from=1-2, to=3-1]
	\arrow["{g_i}"', maps to, from=1-2, to=3-2]
	\arrow["{g_k}", maps to, from=1-2, to=3-3]
	\arrow["{c_1}"', maps to, from=3-1, to=4-1]
	\arrow[maps to, from=3-2, to=4-2]
	\arrow["{c_k}", maps to, from=3-3, to=4-3]
\end{tikzcd}\]%
\caption{For an engine $\engine{j}$, its behaviour acts on a message $m$ of type
$\msgtype{j}$ and an engine's configuration $\config{j}$
and environment $\localenvval{j}(S_j)$. The pre-conditions, denoted as $g_i$, are
guards that determine which action is executed. The symbols $r_i$ represent
output that feeds into the actions $c_i$. The actions, denoted as $c_i$ return
one of the effects as in \Cref{def:effects}. These effects are executed if their
pre-conditions are satisfied. If no pre-conditions are satisfied, the action is a
null operation, $\noop$.}
\label{fig:effect}
\end{figure}

Given an engine $\engine{j}$, the following definition specifies the guard and action
components of an engine's behaviour.

\begin{definition}[Guard]\label{def:guards}%
A \textbf{guard} is a function that returns a value of a given type $\returntype$ if the
guard is satisfied, and $\none$ otherwise; the \emph{guard predicate} is the
corresponding function that maps to false if and only if the guard maps to $\none$.
The guard is a mapping of type %
\begin{equation}
\msgtype{i} \times \configtype{i} \times \envtype{i}{S_i} \to \Option{\returntype}.
\end{equation}
Recall that $\returntype$ is the specific type of data returned when the guard is
satisfied, and $S_i$ is the type of the engine's local state.
\end{definition}

% On the computational aspects of an engine, the behaviour evaluating the
% different guards, and for each guard, the action to be executed, whenever the
% guard is satisfied, is as follows:

\begin{definition}\label{def:action}%
Given an engine $\engine{j}$, an \textbf{action} acting on data of type
$\returntype$ is a mapping of type %
$R \times \msgtype{j} \times \configtype{j} \times \envtype{j}{S_j} \to \effecttype{j}{S_j}$.
\end{definition}

Then, we compose a guard and an action to form a \emph{guarded action}, which is
the building block of which an engine's behaviour consists of. The separation of
guard and action allows for a more modular design. Consider that one may want to
reuse the guard of an engine in different engines or decouple the logic of the
guard against the action, allowing the pre-conditions to be reused in scenarios
where the action is not applicable.

\begin{definition}[Guarded action]\label{def:guarded-action}%
  Given an engine $\engine{j}$, a \textbf{guarded action} of the engine's
  behaviour is a tuple $\guardedaction{j}$, where:
  \begin{itemize}
    \item $\guard{j}$ is a guard as in \Cref{def:guards} that determines when the
    action $\action{j}$ should execute, and
    \item $\action{j}$ is an action of type $\effecttype{j}{S_j}$ as in
    \Cref{def:effects} that specifies what to execute,
  \end{itemize}
\end{definition}

Together, both the guard and action induce a mapping of type

\begin{equation*}\label{eq:guarded-action}%
\msgtype{j} \times \configtype{j} \times \envtype{j}{S_j} \to \effecttype{j}{S_j}\,.
\end{equation*}

%FIXME: The guard can fail, so shouldn't this be Option{\mathsf{Effect}(S_j)}?

This mapping takes in a message, engine configuration, and environment, and
outputs the effect to execute when the guard is satisfied.

So, upon receiving a message, an engine $\engine{j}$ checks which of its guards is
satisfied, and if so, the engine executes the corresponding action, as
illustrated in \Cref{fig:effect}.

%TODO: comment on the given guard action evaluation strategy in the corresponding op. rule

\begin{remark}\label{rem:turing-completeness}%
A question at this point is whether engines can enable unbounded computation.
Behaviours as introduced in~\cite{art-engines} are Turing complete. To see this,
we need to show that the design satisfies the two fundamental requirements:
conditional branching through guard-state interactions and indefinite looping
via message-passing recursion. Note that the message-passing mechanism says
nothing about forbidding engines from sending messages to themselves, which is a
powerful mechanism that allows for recursive patterns. So, while individual
effects as listed in \Cref{def:effects} lack explicit looping constructs, and it
may not seem there is a recursive mechanism at first glance, the combination of
guarded actions acting as conditional statements with open-ended message passing
enables computational universality.
\end{remark}

\begin{definition}[Engine behaviour]\label{def:engine-behaviour}
Given an engine $\engine{j}$, the type of its behaviour is denoted with
$\behaviourtype{i}$, and it is the set of its guarded actions.
\end{definition}

\subsection{Engines}\label{sec:processing-engines}

Having defined the components that make up an engine's behaviour, we can now
formally specify engines themselves. As the fundamental computational units of
our system, engines encapsulate both the processing logic and state management
capabilities. We have established the types of their constituent parts, that is,
guards, actions, configurations and environments. 

\begin{definition}[Engine]\label{def:engine}%
An \textbf{engine} is the computational unit of the system, formally specified
by a tuple $\engineval{i}{b}{s}{\mathsf{cfg}}{\mathsf{env}}$ where:
\begin{itemize}
\item $i$ is the engine's message interface, a type as described in \Cref{def:message-interface},
\item $b$ is the engine's behaviour ---a set of guarded actions as defined in \Cref{def:guarded-action},
\item $s$ is the engine's status flag as specified in \Cref{def:engine-status},
\item $\mathsf{cfg}$ is the engine's configuration as described in
\Cref{def:engine-configuration}, and
\item $\mathsf{env}$ is the engine's execution environment as outlined in \Cref{def:engine-environment}.
\end{itemize}
\end{definition}

% \begin{remark}\label{rem:engine-init}
% No need to initialize an engine. Once declared, an engine is ready to process
% messages.
% \end{remark}

Our system defines two engine types: \emph{processing engines} and \emph{mailbox
engines}. Users can only explicitly create processing engines, while mailbox
engines are automatically created by the system when a processing engine is
instantiated, as detailed in \Cref{sec:creation-rules}. A processing engine is
distinguished by having its operational mode set to $\process$ through
its configuration's mode field. The internals of mailbox engines are described
in \Cref{def:mailbox-engine}.

\begin{definition}[Mailbox engine]\label{def:mailbox-engine}
A \emph{mailbox engine} $\mailbox{j}$ is a system-pre-configured engine
automatically created when instantiating a processing engine $\engine{j}$. It must
satisfy:
\begin{itemize}
\item Operational mode set to $\mail$ (per \Cref{def:engine-mode})
\item Parent reference pointing to its paired processing engine $\engine{j}$
\item Fixed message interface with one message type:
\begin{equation*}
  \typedef%
    {\msgtype{\mailbox{j}}}
    {\append(\msgtype{j})}
\end{equation*}
carrying a payload of type $\msgtype{j}$
\item State $q$ of type $S$ implementing a queue-like structure with:
  \begin{itemize}
  \item $\emptyset$ denoting empty state.
  \item Message insertion to support $\append\mbox{-}$messages, %
  $\typing{\insertmsgop}{S \times \msgtype{j} \to S}.$
  \item Message delation to support internal system delation, %
  $\typing{\deletemsgop}{S \times \msgtype{j} \to S}.$
  % for verification, one might want to add proof that the operations
  % actually insert/delete the element from the structure
  \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}
\end{definition}

\noindent With mailbox engines added to the system, we have a strict producer-consumer
pattern introduced through explicit message-reception semantics, decoupling
message storage from processing logic. This allows for independent scaling of
message ingestion and core computation.
%TODO: add a reference to the corresponing rules in the operational semantics

\subsection{Nodes}\label{sec:nodes}

Having previously introduced the system $\SystemState$, which comprises a set of nodes%
~$\SetOfNodes$ that host engines $\SetOfEngines$ governed by a message-passing
mechanism defined by the system's requirements, we now formally define what
constitutes a node.

\begin{definition}[Node]\label{def:node}%
A \textbf{node} in $\SetOfNodes$ is a tuple 
$\nodeval{n_i}{\enginemap_i}{\plugins_i}$ where $\node{i}$ corresponds to the node's
unique identifier across the system, $\enginemap_i$ is a mapping from
identifiers to engine instances on the node, and $\plugins_i$ represents
node-specific functionality, including, for example, storage and freshness of
unique engine identifiers.
\end{definition}

For convenience, given a node with identifier $n_i$, we have the following notation:
\begin{itemize}
  \item $\SetOfEngines_i$ is the set of engines on the node, which are precisely
  the values of the mapping $\enginemap_i$, and
  \item $\alpha_i$ is a mapping from engine instances into
  their identifiers.
\end{itemize}

\begin{remark}[Modification of nodes and engines]\label{rem:node-operations}
%By design, nodes cannot create, destroy, or modify engines directly.
The creation, destruction, and modification of engines will be \todo{describe in
a phrase} covered in \todo{ref}. 
% J:to consider, if an engine is destroyed, is its mailbox engine also destroyed?
% also, what does it implies if an engine is modified? What can be modified any
% way?
These
operations require global coordination through system-level mechanisms rather
than node-local decisions. This architectural constraint exists because nodes
lack awareness of critical system-wide concerns like address registry management,
topology relationships between components, and coordination mechanisms for
distributed state consistency.

%TODO: introduce a forward reference to the related op. rules
\end{remark}

\subsection{System state}\label{sec:state}%
Having defined the concept of nodes, we now define the overall system. A system
state consists of a collection of nodes and a set of in-transit messages that
represent ongoing communication between engines. The system is a variation of a
Kripke structure. We therefore define the possible system states, \emph{the
possible worlds}, and then specify the transition rules that govern its evolution,
one labelled transition at a time.
%TODO: Review this one more time
% When referecing a "system" we are really
% referencing a "system state". It's confusing.

\begin{definition}[State]\label{def:state} A \textbf{state} of the system
$\SystemState$ is a tuple%
$\equal{%
    \SystemState^i
}{%
\langle
    \SetOfNodes^i
  , \SetOfMessages^i
  , \SetOfPlugins^i
\rangle
}$ where,
\begin{itemize}
\item $\SetOfNodes^i$ is the set of nodes,
\item $\SetOfMessages^i$ is the set of in-transit messages representing ongoing communications, and
\item $\SetOfPlugins^i$ is the set of system-wide services and functions.
\end{itemize}
\end{definition}

% TODO: what are the implications that  \mu^i is a set?
% for the append rule, we require to (i) access a message in the set, and (ii)
% remove it from the set. However, it's not clear if we should preserve the order
% of the messages in the set. If needed, a timestamp could be added to the messages
% to preserve the order.

\begin{remark}\label{rem:system-time-notation}%
The superscript $i$ in $\SystemState^i$ denotes an instantiation of the system.
This can be viewed as a snapshot of the system at a point in time denoted by
index $i$, with the ability to evolve to a successor state $\SystemState^{i+1}$.
\end{remark}

The \emph{initial state} of the system is denoted by $\SystemState^0$. In principle,
we could define the initial state as %
$\defequal{\SystemState^0}{\systemval{\emptyset}{\emptyset}{\emptyset}}$, %
no nodes, no messages, and no services. Via primitives that we will define
later, we can step from the initial state to a successor state with nodes,
messages, and capabilities.

\begin{remark}\label{rem:system-model}%
The Engine Model \cite{art-engines}, which
inspires the system described here, is a communication-based system model
incorporating temporal aspects and dynamic process creation. Here we retain only
the dynamic process creation capability, leaving temporal considerations for
future work. Like \textsc{DETCs}, we use labelled transition system semantics
where transitions occur when processes react to messages by updating their
state, generating new messages and engines. The associated system in
\textsc{DETC} is defined in terms of active processes and a set of messages,
assuming all messages eventually get delivered. A similar approach is adopted
here for the state in \Cref{def:state}, and we adopt as principle that all
messages are eventually delivered.%, see \Cref{lem:message-delivery}.
\end{remark}

The system must provide primitives to aid in the creation of different
entities such as nodes and engines. The system provides the following functions:

\begin{itemize}
\item \freshidname{} is the function that
generates a fresh identifier for node creation.
% \item $\mathsf{freshName}$ is the function that
% generates a fresh name for an engine.
\item $\typing{\mailboxOfname}{\Address \to \Address}$ is the function that
outputs the address of the dedicated mailbox engine for a given processing
engine.
% \begin{equation*}
% \begin{aligned}
%   &\mailboxOfname : \Address \to \Address\\
%   &\mailboxOfname(\langle n_i, \mathsf{id}_j \rangle) \equiv \langle n_i, \mathsf{id}_j \rangle
% \end{aligned}
% \end{equation*}
% TODO: check if this is used somewhere else
\item $\execEffect$ is the function that executes effects as in \Cref{def:effects}
and outputs the new system state after the effect has been executed.
\end{itemize}

We could also extend the node-specific function $\enginemap_i$ to construct a
global version $\enginemap$ that operates over all engines instances
$\SetOfEngines$ in $\SystemState^i$. Recall $\SetOfEngines_i$ is the collection
of engine instances $\SetOfEngines_i$ for $i$ ranging over the node set. This
allows us to look up addresses for any engine in the system regardless of which
node it runs.

Not described here are the functions for removing, and modifying nodes.
Also, one may want to move engines between nodes. We leave these considerations
for future work.

\section{Operational Semantics}\label{sec:semantics}

The transitions of the associated state machine of the system define both how the
system evolves and how the message-passing mechanism functions.

We first recall a few notation conventions. $\SetOfNodes$ denotes the set of
nodes in the system, $\enginemap$ denotes a mapping from engine addresses to
their corresponding instances. $\SetOfEngines$ is the set of values of
$\enginemap$, that is the set of engine instances in the system. The function
$\alpha$ maps engine instances back to their addresses, while $\SetOfMessages$
represents the set of all in-transit messages.

The operational semantics for the system are defined here
via the reduction relation denoted by $\stepsymbol$, where %
$\sysstep{\SystemState^i}{\SystemState^{i+1}}$ signifies that the system state
$\SystemState^i$ evolves to $\SystemState^{i+1}$. The notation $\SystemState^i$
is mentioned in \Cref{rem:system-time-notation}.

% Jonathan: I'm here.

\subsection{System-level initialisation}\label{sec:creation-rules}

In the following rules, we assume that $\SystemState$ is of the form %
$(\SetOfNodes, \SetOfMessages, \SetOfPlugins)$, unless otherwise specified.

\begin{definition}[\srule{Node}]\label{rule:s-Node}
\sloppy~\hfill\\[-\baselineskip]%\hspace{-1.5em}
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$%
      \defequal{\iota}{\freshid}
\quad %
      \equal{%
        n
      }{%
        \nodeval%
          {\iota}
          {\emptyset}
          {\plugins_n}
      }
$}
\UnaryInfC{$%
\opstep%
{\SystemState}%
{node}%
{%
  ( \SetOfNodes \cup \{n\}
  , \SetOfMessages
  , \SetOfPlugins
  )
}%
$}
\end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

\subsection{Engine creation}\label{sec:engine-creation-rules}

To create an engine in the system on node $\node{i}$, we require a
configuration $\mathsf{cfg}$ and an environment $\mathsf{env}$, at least.
Additionally, the new engine must have a registered message interface and an
associated behaviour $b$ that defines how it processes messages, and to be in
ready state, the filter function $f$ must be the identity function.

\begin{definition}[\srule{EngineSpawn}]\label{rule:s-EngineSpawn}
\hspace{-1.5em}
\begin{prooftree}
% Level 1: Node and configuration setup
\AxiomC{$%
\equal{%
  \node{i}
}{%
  \nodeval%
    {\mathsf{id}_i}
    {\enginemap_i}
    {\plugins_i}
}
\in \SetOfNodes%
% \quad m : \mathsf{ \mathsf{EngineMode}} %
\quad \typing{p}{\mathsf{Option}(\Address)} %
\quad %
      \equal{%
        \mathsf{cfg}%
      }{%
        \configval{p}{\process}
      }%
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
      \typing{S}{\U} %
\quad \typing{\mathsf{env}}{\Env(S)}
\quad \typing{in}{\Msg} %
\quad %
      \equal{%
        \engine{\mathsf{new}}
      }{
        \engineval%
          {in} %
          {b} %
          {\ready{\cdots}} %
          {\mathsf{cfg}} %
          {\mathsf{env}}
      }
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
      \typing{\iota}{\freshid}%
\quad \typing{\eta}{\freshid} %
\quad %
      \equal{%
        \mathsf{cfg}_{\mailboxOfname}
      }{%
        \configval%
          {\alpha_i(\engine{\mathsf{new}})} %
          {\mathsf{mail}}
      }%
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
\equal{%
  \mailbox{\mathsf{new}}
}{%
  \engineval%
    {\interface{\mailboxOfname}} %
    {\behaviour{\mailboxOfname}} %
    {\status{\mathsf{ready}(\none)}} %
    {\config{\mailboxOfname}} %
    {\environment{\mailboxOfname}}%
  }%
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
\equal{%
  \enginemap'
}{
  \enginemap_i%
    [\iota \mapsto \engine{\mathsf{new}} ]%
    [\eta  \mapsto \mailbox{\mathsf{new}}] 
}% 
$}
\UnaryInfC{$%
\opstep%
  { \SystemState
  , \node{i}
  , c % the configuration of the engine
  , s % the local environment of the engine
  }
  {spawn}%
  {%
    ( \SetOfNodes[\enginemap_i \mapsto \enginemap']%
    , \SetOfMessages%
    , \SetOfPlugins%
    )
  }%
$}
\end{prooftree}

The terms%
$\interface{\mailboxOfname}$, %
$\behaviour{\mailboxOfname}$, and %
$\environment{\mailboxOfname}$
are the message interface, the behaviour, and the environment of the mailbox engine,
respectively, which follow the specification as in \Cref{def:mailbox-engine}
and when no provided, default values are used.
% TODO: it may seems the function to get mailbox address needs to be defined
% in the node-specific operations.
\end{definition}

\begin{remark}
The rule \sruleref{EngineSpawn} takes place in the context of a node $\node{i}$. When defining a
processing engine $\engine{\mathsf{new}}$, the system also creates a mailbox engine on the same
node, $\mailbox{\mathsf{new}}$. While the only parameters required by this rule are the
processing engine parameters (configuration and environment) for $\engine{\mathsf{new}}$,
one could customize the mailbox parameters, such as its behaviour $\behaviourtype{\mathsf{new}}$,
to enable specialised message routing or priority-based message organisation.
However, for simplicity, we use a uniform mailbox behaviour across all mailbox
engines.
\end{remark}

\begin{comment}
We could think of adding rules for removing nodes and engines. However,
one question just arises to me: how to handle broken references to those
engines that are removed?
\end{comment}

\subsection{Engine garbage collector}\label{sec:engine-termination}

To prevent \emph{zombie} engine instances that could  violate liveness and
safety guarantees, we need to remove terminated engines from the system. This is
done via the \sruleref{Clean} rule.

\begin{definition}[\srule{Clean}]\label{rule:s-Clean}
\hspace{-1.5em}
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$%
  e \in \SetOfEngines%
\quad %
  \propequal{%
    \terminated
  }{%
    \status{e}
  }
$}
\UnaryInfC{$%
\opstep%
  {\SystemState}%
  {clean}%
  {%
    ( \SetOfNodes \setminus \{e\}%
    , \SetOfMessages% TODO: Do we NEED to remove references to e???
    , \SetOfPlugins%
    )
  }%
$}
\end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

\begin{remark}
Notice how the \sruleref{Clean} rule does not remove the engine's mailbox.
Even when removing the processing engine, the mailbox is kept in the system
receiving messages, but not being processed. This ensures that messages are not
lost.
%Also, the mailbox can be reused for future engine instances? as it is a matter
%of changing the engine's configuration.
\end{remark}

\subsection{Message-passing mechanism}\label{sec:message-passing-mechanism}

Our message-passing mechanism aims to support (i) asynchronous communication
with non-blocking sends, (ii) mailbox isolation with guaranteed eventual
delivery, and (iii) explicit lifecycle management for engines.


\begin{definition}[\mrule{Send}]\label{rule:m-Send}
\hspace{-1.5em}
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$%
\engine{s}, \engine{t} \in \SetOfEngines%
\quad %
  \typing{v}{\msgtype{t}}%
\quad %
  \equal{%
    \SystemState
  }{%
    (\SetOfNodes, \SetOfMessages, \SetOfPlugins)
  }%
$}
\UnaryInfC{$%
\opstep%
  {%
    \SystemState, \alpha(\engine{t}), v%
  }
  {send}%
  {%
    ( \SetOfNodes
    , \SetOfMessages \cup%
      \{ 
        \msgpacket%
          {\alpha(\engine{s})}
          {\mailboxOf{\enginemap(\engine{t})}}
          {v}
      \}%
    , \SetOfPlugins%
    )
  }%
$}
\end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

The \mrule{Send} rule above supports the sending of messages between engines in
the context of a system state $\SystemState$. When engine $\engine{s}$ sends a message to
engine $\engine{t}$, the rule handles the registration of the message in $\engine{t}$'s
dedicated mailbox. This message must be well-typed according to $\engine{t}$'s message
interface specification, and it is the only way to add a message to $\SetOfMessages$.

%TODO: add a lemma stating that the operational mode
% of an engine found in the destination of a message is
% always an mailbox.

Now, with a message in-transit, the system needs to deliver it to the target engine's
mailbox. This is done via the \mruleref{Enqueue} rule.

\begin{definition}[\mrule{Enqueue}]\label{rule:m-Enqueue}
\hspace{-1.5em}
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$%
\equal{%
  m
}{%
  \msgpacket%
    {\cdots}
    {\mailbox{t}} 
    {\append(v)}
} \in \SetOfMessages
\quad %
  \equal{%
    q
  }{%
    \mathsf{env}(\enginemap(\mailbox{t}))
  }%
$}
%
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$
\equal{%
  \eta
}{%
  \mathsf{fst}(\mailbox{t})
}
\quad %
  \equal{%
    \enginemap'
  }{%
    \enginemap[%
      \mailbox{t}
        \mapsto%
          \langle%
            \cdots,%
            q \cup \{v\} %
          \rangle%
    ]%
  }
$}
\UnaryInfC{$%
\opstep%
  {\SystemState}
  {enqueue}
  {%
    ( \SetOfNodes[ \enginemap_\eta \mapsto \enginemap' ]
    , \SetOfMessages \setminus \{m\}
    , \SetOfPlugins%
    )
  }
$}
\end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\mrule{Dequeue}]\label{rule:m-Dequeue}
\hspace{-1.5em}
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$
  \engine{p} \in \SetOfMessages%
\quad %
  \equal{
    \engine{p}
  }{%
      \langle%
      \cdots,
      \ready{\cdots}, %
      \cdots%
      \rangle
  }
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$
  \mailbox{p} \in \SetOfEngines%
\quad %
  \equal{
    \mailbox{p}
  }{%
    \mailboxOf{\engine{p}}
  }
\quad %
  \propequal{%
    \mathsf{env}(\enginemap(\mailbox{p}))
  }{%
   q \cup \{v\}
  }
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
\equal{%
  \SetOfNodes'
}{%
  \SetOfNodes[%
    \enginemap(\engine{p})%
      \mapsto%
        \langle %
          \cdots, %
          \busy{v}, %
          \cdots %
        \rangle
    ]%
    [\mailbox{p}
        \mapsto 
          \langle %
            \cdots %
            , q %
          \rangle %
    ]%
}
$}
\UnaryInfC{$%
\opstep%
  {\SystemState}
  {dequeue}
  {
    ( \SetOfNodes'
    , \SetOfMessages
    , \SetOfPlugins
    )
  }
$}
\end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

After a processing engine retrieves a message via the \mruleref{Dequeue}
rule, it enters the \texttt{busy} state to handle the incoming message. This
message processing involves executing the engine's predefined behaviour through
the \sruleref{Process} rule, as defined in~\Cref{sec:engine-processing}.
However, we need first to establish how an engine's behaviour evaluates messages
through its guarded actions, and how the evaluation of those effects changes the
system state.

\subsection{Behaviour evaluation}\label{sec:behavior-eval-rules}

\begin{comment}
restrict attention to machines
whose behaviour is guaranteed to terminate,
\end{comment}

While the system's operational semantics primarily govern inter-engine
communication, individual engines retain full autonomy over the computations 
that they perform locally to process messages.
This subsection formalises the evaluation mechanism for
guarded actions that govern engine responses to messages. Specifically, the
following rules define the process by which an engine $p$ consumes a message $v$
from its mailbox, formalised via, first the judgment

\begin{equation}\label{eq:judgement}
\evalstep%
  {p}%
  {v}%
  {E}%
,%
\end{equation}
that is, processing the message $v$ within the context of a busy engine $p$,
results in an effect $E$, and second, a more fine-grained judgment
of the former to introduce the guarded action used to produce the effect.

\begin{equation}\label{eq:guarded-action-eval}
  \guardevalmsgstep%
    {p}%
    {g}%
    {a}%
    {v}%
    {E}%
  ,
\end{equation}
%
states that in the context of engine $p$, the guarded action%
$\langle g, a \rangle$ produces the effect $E$ when processing the message $v$.

\begin{definition}[\brule{GuardedActionEval}]\label{rule:b-GuardedActionEval}
\hspace{-1.5em}
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$%
  p \in \SetOfEngines%
\quad %
  \equal{%
    p
  }{%  
    \engineval%
      {\cdots}
      {b_p}
      {\busy{v}}
      {\config{p}}
      {\localenv{p}}
  }
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
  \guardedactionval%
    {g}
    {a}
  \in \behaviour{p}
\quad %
  \propequal{%
    g(v, \config{p}, \localenv{p})
  }{%
    \some{r}
  }
$}
\UnaryInfC{$
\guardevalmsgstep%
  {p}
  {g}
  {a}
  {v}
  {a(r, v, \config{p}, \localenv{p})}
$}
\end{prooftree}
\end{definition}


\begin{definition}[\brule{NoMatchingGuard}]\label{rule:b-NoMatchingGuard}
\hspace{-1.5em}
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$%
  p \in \SetOfEngines%
\quad %
  \equal{%
    p
  }{
    \engineval%
      {\cdots}
      {\behaviour{p}}
      {\cdots}
      {\config{p}}
      {\localenv{p}}
  }
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
\guardedactionval{g}{a} \in \behaviour{p}%
\quad %
  \propequal{%
    g(v, \config{p}, \localenv{p})
   }{%
    \none%
   }
$}
\UnaryInfC{$%
\guardevalmsgstep%
  {p}
  {g}
  {a}
  {v}
  {\noop}
$}
\end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

\paragraph{Guard selection strategy}
Behaviours consist of a set of guarded actions. Execution of this behaviour
requires a strategy to select which guarded action to execute. For the sake of
simplicity, we assume that the strategy is deterministic, and that the engine
runs an action only if it is the only one that matches the message. Again, other
guard selection strategies may cause unintended behaviour, and the study of which
strategies are valid, and their semantics is out of scope of this paper.

\begin{definition}[\brule{GuardStrategy}]\label{rule:b-GuardStrategy}
\hspace{-1.5em}
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$%
p \in \SetOfEngines%
\quad %
  \equal{%
    p
  }{%
    \engineval%
      {\ignore}
      {\behaviour{p}}
      {\busy{v}}
      {\ignore}
      {\ignore}
  }
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
  \guardedaction{i} \in \behaviour{p}%
% \quad p , \langle g_i, a_i \rangle \vdash v \downarrow E
\quad %
  \guardevalmsgstep%
    {p}
    {\guard{i}}
    {\action{i}}
    {v}
    {E}
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
\forall_{j\neq i} 
  \guardedaction{j} \in \behaviour{p} %
  \,.\
  \guardevalmsgstep%
  {p}
  {\guard{j}}
  {\action{j}}
  {v}
  {\noop}
$}  
\UnaryInfC{$%
\evalstep%
  {p}
  {v}
  {E}
$}
\end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

\begin{remark}
Notice that there is no rule here that allows two guards to match the same
message, that is, producing different non-noop effects. This is an example of
unexpected behaviour that a different guard strategy could refine.
\end{remark}

\subsection{Effect execution}\label{sec:effect-execution}

With rules how to get the effect resulting from processing a message, we now
need to state how the system evolves when an effect is executed.

Given a system state $\SystemState$, an effect $E$ and a new system state
$\SystemState'$, we write 
\begin{equation}\label{eq:effect-exec}
\effectevalstep%
  {\SystemState}
  {E}%
  {\SystemState'}
\end{equation}
to state that when the effect $E$ is executed in the system state
$\SystemState$, it produces the new system state $\SystemState'$. As the effect
$E$ takes many forms, this judgment is defined by case analysis on the effect
$E$, as shown in the following definitions.


\begin{definition}[\erule{Send}]\label{rule:e-Send}
\hspace{-1.5em}
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$%
  \engine{s}, \engine{t} \in \SetOfEngines%
\quad %
  \equal%
    {
      l_{t} % address
    }{%
      \alpha(\engine{t})
    }
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
      \typing{v}{\msgtype{s}}
\quad \typing{m}{\msgtype{t}}
\quad %
     \evalstep%
        {\engine{s}}
        {v}
        {\send{l_{t}}{m}}
\quad %
      \sendstep%
        {\SystemState}
        {l_{t}}
        {m}
        {\SystemState'}
$}
\UnaryInfC{$
\effectevalstep%
  {\SystemState}
  {\send{l_t}{m}}
  {\SystemState'}
$}
\end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\erule{Update}]\label{rule:e-Update}
\hspace{-1.5em}
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$%
      \engine{i} \in \SetOfEngines%
\quad \typing{S}{\U}%
\quad \typing%
        {
          \mathsf{env}(\engine{i}) , s
        }{
          \envtype{i}{S}
        }%
$}
\UnaryInfC{$%
\effectevalstep%
  {(\SetOfNodes, \cdots)}
  {\update{s}}
  {(\SetOfNodes[
    \enginemap(\engine{i}) \mapsto %
      \langle%
        \cdots
      , s%
      \rangle
    ],%
   \cdots)}
$}
\end{prooftree}
\end{definition}


\begin{definition}[\erule{MFilter}]\label{rule:e-MFilter}
  \hspace{-1.5em}
  \begin{prooftree}
  \AxiomC{$%
  \quad \engine{i} \in \SetOfEngines%
  \quad \typing{f}{\Option{\msgtype{i} \to \Bool}}%
  \quad \evalstep%
            {\engine{i}}
            {v}
            {\mfilter{f}}%
  $}
  \UnaryInfC{$%
  \effectevalstep%
  {(\SetOfNodes, \cdots)}
  {\mfilter{f}}
  {(\SetOfNodes[
    \enginemap(\engine{i}) \mapsto %
      \langle%
        \cdots
      , \ready{f}%
      , \cdots
      \rangle
    ],%
  \cdots)}
$}
  \end{prooftree}
  \end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\erule{Spawn}]\label{rule:e-Spawn}
\hspace{-1.5em}
\begin{prooftree}
% parent engine
\AxiomC{$%
\node{i} \in \SetOfNodes%
\quad p \in \SetOfEngines_i%
\quad %
  \equal%
    {
      c
    }{
      \configval%
        {\alpha(p)}
        {\process}
    }
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
      \typing{S}{\U}%
\quad \typing{s}{\envtype{j}{S}}% %FIXME: ENV? which
\quad %
      \spawnstep%
        {\SystemState}
        {\node{i}}
        {c}
        {s}
        {\SystemState'}%
$}
\UnaryInfC{$%
  \effectevalstep%
    {\SystemState}
    {\spawn{c}{s}}
    {\SystemState'}
$}
\end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\erule{Chain}]\label{rule:e-Chain}
\hspace{-1.5em}
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$%
      p \in \SetOfEngines%
\quad v \in \SetOfMessages%
\quad \typing{v}{\msgtype{p}}%
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
     \evalstep%
        {p}
        {v}
        {\chain{\mathit{eff}_1}{\mathit{eff}_2}}%
\quad \effectevalstep%
        {\SystemState}
        {\mathit{eff}_1}
        {\SystemState'}
\quad \effectevalstep%
        {\SystemState'}
        {\mathit{eff}_2}
        {\SystemState''}
$}
\UnaryInfC{$%
\effectevalstep%
  {\SystemState }
  {\chain{\mathit{eff}_1}{\mathit{eff}_2}}
  {\SystemState''}
$}
\end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\erule{Terminate}]\label{rule:e-Terminate}
\hspace{-1.5em}
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$%
      p \in \SetOfEngines%
\quad v \in \SetOfMessages%
\quad \typing{v}{\msgtype{p}}%
\quad \evalstep%
          {p}
          {v}
          {\terminate}%
\quad \opstep%
          {\SystemState}
          {\mathsf{clean}}
          {\SystemState'}
$}
\UnaryInfC{$%
\effectevalstep
  {\SystemState}
  {\terminate}
  {\SystemState'}
$}
\end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\erule{Noop}]\label{rule:e-Noop}
  \hspace{-1.5em}
  \begin{prooftree}
  \AxiomC{$%
        p \in \SetOfEngines%
  \quad v \in \SetOfMessages%
  \quad \typing{v}{\msgtype{p}}%
  \quad \evalstep%
          {p}
          {v}
          {\noop}
  $}
  \UnaryInfC{$%
  \effectevalstep%
    {\SystemState}
    {\noop}
    {\SystemState}
  $}
  \end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

\subsection{Engine processing}\label{sec:engine-processing}

This section introduces the core system-level operational rule
\sruleref{Process} that governs message processing by engines
denoted as follows. Given a system state $\SystemState$, an engine $p$ and a message
$m \in \SetOfMessages$,
the judgment
\begin{equation}\label{eq:process-judgment}
\processstep%
  {\SystemState}
  {p}
  {m}
  {\SystemState'}
\end{equation}
reads as with system state $\SystemState$, when engine $p$ processes the message $m$,
it produces a new system state $\SystemState'$. This rule aims to coordinate three
critical aspects:
\begin{enumerate*}[label=(\roman*)]
  \item effect generation from message processing, and
  \item engine state transitions from \textsf{busy} to \textsf{ready} after successful execution.
\end{enumerate*}

\begin{definition}[\srule{Process}]\label{rule:s-Process}
\hspace{-1.5em}
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$%
      \node{i} \in \SetOfNodes%
\quad p \in \SetOfEngines_i%
\quad %
      \equal{
        p 
      }{
        \engineval%
          {\interface{p}}
          {\behaviour{p}}
          {\busy{m}}
          {\config{p}}
          {\localenv{p}} 
      }
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
      \evalstep%
        {p}
        {m}
        {E}
\qquad \effectevalstep%
          {\SystemState}
          {E}
          {(\SetOfNodes', \SetOfNodes', \SetOfPlugins')}
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
\equal{
  \enginemap'
}{
  \enginemap[ %
    \alpha(p) %
      \mapsto %
        \engineval%
          {\interface{p}}
          {\behaviour{p}}
          {\ready{!!!!!}}
          {\config{p}}
          {\localenv{p}}
    ]
}
$}
\UnaryInfC{$%
\processstep%
  {\SystemState}
  {p}
  {m}
  {( \SetOfNodes'[%
        \enginemap \mapsto \enginemap'
        ]%
    , \SetOfMessages'
    , \SetOfPlugins'
  )}
$}
\end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

\subsection{System state transition rules}

The inference rules from previous sections collectively govern system state
transitions. We establish the state transition type by showing that system-level
judgments in \Cref{fig:state-transition-rules} induce state transitions:

\begin{equation}
\opstep%
  {\SystemState, \cdots}
  {op}
  {\SystemState'}%
\quad \text{implies}%
\quad \sysstep%
        {\SystemState}
        {\SystemState'} 
\end{equation}

\noindent for any system-level atomic operation $\mathsf{op}$.
System-level operations are labelled with identifiers like \srule{Name} in \Cref{fig:state-transition-rules}.

\begin{figure}[h]
  \centering
  \begin{tabular}{ll}
    \textbf{Category} & \textbf{Rules} \\ \hline
    System operations &  
        \sruleref{Node},
        \sruleref{EngineSpawn},
        \sruleref{Clean},
        \sruleref{Process} \\
    Message passing & 
        \mruleref{Send},
        \mruleref{Enqueue},
        \mruleref{Dequeue} \\
    Behaviour evaluation & 
        \bruleref{GuardedActionEval},
        \bruleref{NoMatchingGuard},
        \bruleref{GuardStrategy} \\
    Effect execution & 
        \eruleref{Send},
        \eruleref{Update},
        \eruleref{MFilter},
        \eruleref{Spawn},
        \eruleref{Chain},
        \eruleref{Terminate},
        \eruleref{Noop}
  \end{tabular}
  \caption{Rules for the operational semantics categorised by domain.}
  \label{fig:state-transition-rules}
\end{figure}

\begin{remark}
Critical dependencies exist between these rules: \mruleref{Send} enables
inter-engine communication but requires \mruleref{Enqueue} for message
delivery. The \sruleref{Process} rule depends on successful message
consumption via \mruleref{Dequeue} and the related judgment for behaviour
evaluation, as defined in \Cref{sec:behavior-eval-rules}. On the other hand, the
\sruleref{Clean} rule prevents resource leakage by removing terminated
engines. Omission of these components, that is, system-level or message-passing,
would raise undefined behaviour, for example, excluding \mruleref{Enqueue}
would leave messages undelivered, while removing \sruleref{Clean} would
allow infinite accumulation of dormant engines.
\end{remark}

% This should be provable.
% \begin{lemma}[Eventual reliable message delivery]\label{ax:message-delivery}%
%   For any message in transit within
%   system state $\SystemState$, there exists a subsequent state $\SystemState'$ where $m$ is
%   eventually delivered to its intended recipient.
% \end{lemma}
  
\section{Concluding remarks}\label{sec:related}

The Engine Model~\cite{art-engines}, which serves as our focus within the Actor
model paradigm, shares conceptual ground with Erlang's process-oriented
architecture and similar Actor model implementations that treat actors as
processes ---entities we refer to as
\emph{engines} in our framework.

Fundamentally, engines represent processes augmented with additional structural
components but not particularly crucial to the main design proposed here. In this
paper, we proposed a mailboxes-as-actor approach that naturally extends to any
Actor-like model that shares a process-oriented
paradigm~\cite{taxonomy-of-actor-models}. 

\paragraph{Related work}
Kilim's framework~\cite{kilim-framework} aligns to the mailbox decoupling, goal
of this paper. Kilim achieves the mailbox separation through specialised queues.
However, Kilim's implementation treats mailboxes as passive data structures
rather than first-class actors. This limitation persists across most Actor model
implementations like Agha's Classic Actor Model~\cite{foundation97agha}, and
SALSA~\cite{taxonomy-of-actor-models}, where mailboxes typically function as
internal components with limited exposed functionality beyond basic queue
operations, or like Erlang, where mailbox interaction remains a low-level system
primitive with opaque handling~\cite{erlang-2010}.

In short, by promoting mailboxes to opaque first-class actors, we combine
Kilim's structural separation with Erlang's process-oriented operational
transparency, plus the ability to implement custom mailbox behaviours.
These mailbox engines act as \emph{secretaries} ---intelligent brokers that can
filter content, prioritize messages, delay delivery, or debug issues through
configurable rules.

\paragraph{Multiple mailboxes per processing actor}
While our architecture could naturally accommodate multiple mailbox actors per
processing actor, a pattern that might enable specialised message routing or
priority queues, we consciously omit this extension from our core design. This
restriction serves to preserve the system's verifiable properties. Concurrent
pulling from multiple mailboxes could introduce race conditions that violate our
exactly-once delivery guarantee. The coordination overhead required for atomic
operations across distributed mailboxes would also compromise the simplicity
benefits of our decoupled architecture. Therefore, we intentionally maintain a
one-to-one relationship between processing actors and their mailboxes, as this
constraint ensures reliable message ordering while preserving the opacity and
encapsulation benefits central to our design.

\paragraph{System properties}
There is nothing that prevents us from proving system properties for this
extended model. A possible starting point would be to add a timeout primitive to
the engine model and demonstrate that the system remains live and makes progress
even in the presence of timeouts for practical implementations.

\paragraph{Practical implementations}
While several systems implement related concepts, no existing implementations
fully adopt the mailbox-as-actor design. Actor frameworks like Kilim for
Java~\cite{kilim-framework} demonstrate partial alignment by decoupling
mailboxes from actors ---treating them as object/work-queues where actors pull
messages when ready to process them. We identify analogous approaches in the
JavaScript ecosystem, such as the WeChaty library employed in the industrial chatbot
applications using actor-like architectures~\cite{wechaty}.
Our work's distinct contribution
lies in formally promoting mailboxes to first-class citizens, actors, within the
model itself, rather than treating them as implementation details or external
components.

\paragraph{Rules as way to guide implementation}
The operational semantics given in \Cref{sec:semantics} can be seen as a way to
guide the implementation of the \say{mailbox-as-an-actor} approach, described in this
paper. We presented the rules in such a way that they should be easy to reason about and
implement.

The system-level rules are given in \Cref{sec:creation-rules}. In this section,
we described how the system grows from the initial empty state, with the addition
of nodes, engines, and later on messages in \Cref{sec:message-passing-mechanism}. We
cover other rules such as \srule{Clean} as a garbage-collector for engines,
however, we did not cover rules for on-demand engine or node
deletion/disconnection. This can be seen as an extension of the model.

Our message-passing mechanism implements the rules given in
\Cref{sec:message-passing-mechanism}. The \mrule{Send} rule enables asynchronous
message realisation in the system, allowing concurrent message generation
without sender blocking. Messages are reliably delivered to mailbox engines
through the \mrule{Enqueue} rule, which implements atomic \say{enqueuing}.
Message digestion is handled by the \mrule{Dequeue} rule.

Finally, and probably one of the most interesting rules, the \srule{Process}
rule enables atomic state transitions by combining behaviour evaluation, rules
in \Cref{sec:behavior-eval-rules} with effect handling, and rules in
\Cref{sec:effect-execution}. Together, the rules in \Cref{sec:semantics} form a
complete system for reliable asynchronous communication with proper resource
management.

\paragraph{Future work}
As mentioned in \Cref{sec:related}, we plan to implement a prototype in Elixir
leveraging its Erlang-compatible actor model support on the BEAM virtual
machine.%
% The prototype source code is available in the Anoma Foundation's public
% repository at \url{https://github.com/anoma/engine}.

Troyer.
